input.initialPrompt: Modernize this project to SDK-style format. Identify all legacy patterns and suggest how to simplify the project file.

data.reasoningText: The user wants me to modernize the .csproj file from the old-style format to the new SDK-style format. Let me first look at the current project file and the source files to understand what we're working with.

arguments.new_str:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>
    <RootNamespace>LegacyApp</RootNamespace>
    <AssemblyName>LegacyApp</AssemblyName>
    <Description>A legacy-style project for modernization testing</Description>
    <Company>TestCo</Company>
    <Product>LegacyApp</Product>
    <Copyright>Copyright © 2024</Copyright>
    <Version>1.0.0.0</Version>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a clear picture of the legacy project. Let me identify the legacy patterns and modernize it to SDK-style format.

Legacy patterns identified:
1. **Old-style `<Project>` element** with `ToolsVersion` and `xmlns` — replaced by `<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">`
2. **Explicit `Import` of `Microsoft.Common.props` and `Microsoft.CSharp.targets`** — handled implicitly by the SDK
3. **Configuration/Platform conditionals** for Debug/Release — SDK defaults handle these
4. **`ProjectGuid`** — no longer needed


5. **`FileAlignment`**, **`AutoGenerateBindingRedirects`**, and **`Deterministic`** — all SDK defaults that can be removed
6. **Debug/Release property groups** — entirely redundant since the SDK provides sensible defaults
7. **Explicit `<Compile>` items and framework assembly references** — the SDK automatically includes all C# files and implicit framework assemblies
8. **`Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs`** — I can consolidate those attributes directly into the project file as package properties, which the SDK auto-generates
9. **`TargetFrameworkVersion v4.7.2`** — converts to `<TargetFramework>net472</TargetFramework>`

The resulting modernized project file will be drastically simplified, and I can delete `AssemblyInfo.cs` entirely since the SDK handles attribute generation from project properties.

    <WarningLevel>4</WarningLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <Reference Include="System" />
    <Reference Include="System.Core" />
    <Reference Include="System.Data" />
    <Reference Include="System.Xml" />
    <Reference Include="System.Xml.Linq" />
    <Reference Include="Microsoft.CSharp" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <Compile Include="Calculator.cs" />
    <Compile Include="StringHelper.cs" />
    <Compile Include="Properties\AssemblyInfo.cs" />
  </ItemGroup>
  <Import Project="$(MSBuildToolsPath)\Microsoft.CSharp.targets" />
</Project>

data.content:
Project modernized successfully. Here's what was simplified:

**Legacy patterns removed:**

| Pattern | Why it's unnecessary in SDK-style |
|---------|----------------------------------|
| `ToolsVersion`, `xmlns` | Replaced by `Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk"` |
| `Import` of Common.props/targets | Implicit in SDK |
| `ProjectGuid` | Not needed |
| `Configuration`/`Platform` conditionals | SDK defaults handle Debug/Release |
| Debug & Release `PropertyGroup`s | All values match SDK defaults |
| `OutputType Library` | Default for SDK class libraries |
| `FileAlignment`, `Deterministic` | SDK defaults |
| Explicit `<Compile>` items | SDK auto-globs `**/*.cs` |
| Framework `<Reference>` items | Implicit for .NET Framework targets |
| `Properties/AssemblyInfo.cs` | Attributes auto-generated from `<PropertyGroup>` metadata |

**Result:** 47 lines → 13 lines. The project restores and builds cleanly with zero warnings.
